feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers European Commission 1049 Bruxelles/Brussel Belgium
24 October 2025
Re: Digital Fairness Act public consultation
Contributors (in alphabetical order):

Ahmed, Osamah (LLM student, Utrecht University) Henriksen, Thomas Ø. (LLM student, Utrecht University) Liaropoulou, Erini (LLM student, Utrecht University) Liptak, Tomas (LLM student, Utrecht University)
To whom it may concern,

This is a response to the solicitation for public comments of the European Commission, relating to the fairness check. This response reflects the discussions of a focus group undertaken as part of the course EU Digital Innovation and Regulation in the context of the Masters Programme Law & Technology in Europe at Utrecht University, where social media influencer/creators have been a focal point.

Our proposal does not aim to define who an influencer/creator should be, however we instead point to what we believe is necessary to consider when it comes to regulating this area. We believe the DFA in assessing influencer/creators and their impact, consideration should be had towards the impact of their content, however, and what our focus is rested on, is the idea that influencer/creators of a certain size should have varying degrees of accountability. This furthers the principle of transparency towards both the influencer/creator and the public. Our focus is not to concretely define the influencer/creators' obligations, but to recognise that there must be tools available to assist influencer/creators to realise their obligations they might (and should) have, informing users about influencer/creator metrics in simplified matters about their level of obligations based on their reach.

We firmly believe that our proposal's goals should be fulfilled in large part by placing obligations on platforms to provide or help to provide such information analytical data. This legislative proposal is meant to be applied in a unifying manner across multiple platforms, so that influencers can see where their obligations lie and know how to act.

We expect this proposal to properly function, given that influencers, as well as the online platforms supporting their content, have certain concrete obligations, such as a) avoiding promotion of harmful products/content to vulnerable groups, b) special moderation or flagging obligations of their content and c) special content labelling obligations, among others.

Moreover, we submit that the DFA should be differentiated from the Unfair commercial practices directive in defining an influencer. In this manner, influencers would not fall as market actors under the UCPD.

What we aim is to clarify whether a content creator is an influencer or not and what obligations this brings for them. Those clarifications are meant for all parties, including influencers and platforms but also the people targeted.
We initially would require an obligation for platforms to provide intercompatible statistical data outputs to content creators regarding their audience.

We believe the DFA should, through a relevant body, create a tool/website that allows you to put your different statistical information to calculate your actual reach, combining analytical data from multiple platforms. This tool/website should be provided by a competent EU body that would ensure transparency, impartiality, and independence of the assessment system. The relevant body, when providing this tool should ensure transparency, this is achievable by defining common methodological criteria for calculating reach, ensuring cross-border consistency of assessments, avoiding national fragmentation, monitoring compliance with the principles of data protection and proportionality
We highlight below relevant considerations around the creation of this tool:
The tool could be, for instance, a website that gives the content creator an estimate of whether they fall into the category of influencer;

This self-checking tool would exist to inform influencers, their audience, and any other relevant actor; the purpose of the tool should not be to penalise them. For example, the tool can say that you show characteristics of an influencer and you potentially fall into the scope of the DFA leaving the obligation on you and to consider the nature of your content and potential effects.

The tool would be available for everyone and would be based on metrics from various platforms, combining the analytical data so an influencer can understand their true reach. The influencer's audience should also be able to use this tool.

The calculation mechanism for reach should be fair and transparent (e.g. publishing the calculation process under a specific segment on the platform, creating a form for complaints or for contesting a calculation made for someone)

The calculation system should provide the same results about an influencer, regardless of the influencer limiting their obligations by creating companies or using other means to "limit" their influencer reach (e.g. by spreading their online personalities across different platforms and companies). The calculation should combine their reach across different platforms, with the purpose of avoiding content creators gaming the DFA.

An example could be a personality hired by a company that has their separate "personal" account with a much smaller audience - the "reach" should be looked at based on the personality and for the enforcement authorities to consider the website/platform, potential company and role within same, their circumstances, etc.
Impact should be a different metric to reach calculation in relation to the potential harm arising from potential breaches.

This position around influencer reach is similar to DMA/DSA and VLOPs/VLOSEs. We must add that there should be guidelines and obligations available for influencers who undergo growth. For example, an obligation around retrospective correction towards their potential harmful content while they were not an influencer.

In cases of influencer movability, for example where they may move to different platforms or different companies, there must be an element of retroactivity, where historical data and past content from the platforms can be used in order to evaluate whether a content creator is an influencer or not
The desired results of our proposal include, but are not limited to:
a) enhanced legal certainty;
b) reduction of the ability of an influencer to deny liability by virtue of claiming ignorance of the legal framework;
c) by virtue of providing the influencer with information, incentivising them both to consider the harms of the products or services they are selling and promoting as well as alerting
them of potential legal obligations they might incur, and when it would be prudent to pursue a more accurate and complete analysis;

d) creating a regulatory framework considering the scenario that an influencer is not malicious, but simply uninformed or unaware of the obligations that they must comply with, as well as the effects of their content, which they should become aware of through the Digital Fairness Act.
